title: Extensible positive loops and vanishing of symplectic cohomology
speaker: Dylan Cant
abstract: I will discuss the relationship between positive loops of contactomorphisms of a fillable contact manifold and the symplectic cohomology (SH) of the filling. The main result is that the existence of a positive loop which is "extensible" implies SH vanishes. We also discuss the relationship between non-extensible loops and exotic mapping classes in the symplectomorphism group. The results have a relative (Lagrangian) formulation: an extensible positive loop of fillable Legendrians implies the wrapped Floer cohomology of the filling vanishes.

proof_stages.label: Ideal-restriction fibration and extensible positive loops
proof_stages.summary: The ideal-restriction map from Hamiltonian isotopies of a Liouville manifold to contact isotopies of the ideal boundary is a Serre fibration, so the central hypothesis is that a positive contact loop lifts to a Hamiltonian loop, with contractible loops as an automatic special case.

proof_stages.label: Fixed-point Floer cohomology and positive continuation
proof_stages.summary: The proof is set up using fixed-point Floer cohomology for Hamiltonian isotopies with compact nondegenerate fixed points, and nonnegative paths at infinity define continuation maps via energy-controlled twisted holomorphic cylinders.

proof_stages.label: Naturality under Hamiltonian loops
proof_stages.summary: Precomposing an isotopy with a Hamiltonian loop based at the identity leaves the time-one map and the twisted Floer moduli spaces unchanged, giving naturality isomorphisms that commute with continuation maps.

proof_stages.label: Twisting lemma and surjectivity to the colimit
proof_stages.summary: After lifting the positive boundary loop, iterating it produces a cofinal positive sequence of Floer groups computing symplectic cohomology, and naturality identifies the sequence with its tails so that every initial Floer group surjects onto the colimit.

proof_stages.label: Unit-birth contradiction and vanishing
proof_stages.summary: If symplectic cohomology were nonzero, its unit would not come from a sufficiently small negative-slope Floer group in the aspherical/exact setting, contradicting the surjectivity lemma and forcing vanishing, subject to the noted quantum-correction caveat in more general settings.

proof_stages.label: Exact relative version for Legendrian loops
proof_stages.summary: The same extensibility idea is translated to a relative Serre-fibration setting for cylindrical exact Lagrangians and their Legendrian ends, yielding vanishing of wrapped Floer cohomology when the Legendrian admits a positive exact extensible loop.

proof_stages.label: Consequences for stabilizations, Reeb chords, and mapping classes
proof_stages.summary: The vanishing results recover the stabilization vanishing theorem, imply a relative Reeb-chord consequence for fillable Legendrians, and via the connecting homomorphism turn nonvanishing symplectic cohomology plus positive loops into nontrivial symplectic mapping classes or exotic fillings.

examples.name: Coordinate rotation on C^n
examples.note: Rotating all complex coordinates gives a positive extensible loop, and the one-fixed-point Floer complexes with grading shifts motivate the expectation that the colimit symplectic cohomology should vanish.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker computes the Floer-theoretic behavior for coordinate rotations of C^n, identifying the origin as the sole generator, tracking grading changes, zero continuation maps, and SH(C^n)=0

examples.name: Persistence-module bar picture
examples.note: The surjectivity lemma is explained by saying that every half-infinite bar in a one-parameter Floer persistence module is already present from the start, while finite bars are irrelevant to the colimit.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the persistence-module bar picture to interpret the lemma, reasoning that no half-infinite bars are born and only finite bars may remain
